coredata.dc:title: Triple channel fluorescence Na-Ca-Cl-doped carbon dots for erythrosine detection in food samples and living cells

coredata.dc:description: It is an ongoing and imperative need to monitor food colorants, for unsafe food is a terrifying threat to human health and economy. Here new Na-Ca-Cl-doped carbon dots (CDs) was fabricated by hydrothermal treatment of Miswak and m-phenylenediamine (mPD). The CDs were green-emitting with three distinctive emission centers, displaying a high quantum yield of 47 %. The CDs were able to assess erythrosine in the triple channels based on their fluorescence quenching by erythrosine because of the inner filter effect, displaying high sensitivity and selectivity. Also, the quantitative measurement of erythrosine utilizing the CDs was realized in food samples and in living cells with admirable fluorescence stability and appealing biocompatibility, representing the first report on exploiting CDs for erythrosine sensing in living cells. Collectively, the CDs provide a simple efficient platform for erythrosine sensing in solutions, food samples, and living cells, broadening the implements of CDs as bioprobes for food colorants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food nanotechnology
Multi-atoms-doped carbon dots
Multicenter emissions
Food colorant
Intracellular detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
Triple channel detection of erythrosine in solutions using the CDs
Detection of erythrosine in different drinks
Imaging and sensing of erythrosine in animal cells using the CDs
Morphology, optical properties, and chemical composition of CDs
Triple-channel fluorescence sensing of erythrosine utilizing the CDs in various solutions with high selectivity
Possible detection mechanism for erythrosine using the CDs
Real sample detection of erythrosine in red wine, grapes juice, beer, and blackcurrant juice
Application of the CDs for intracellular erythrosine detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic demonstration of the hydrothermal fabrication of multi-emission centers CDs from Miswak and m-phenylenediamine (mPD), and its application for triple channel erythrosine detection in solutions, food samples, and living cells.

Characteristics of the CDs. (A) The CDs’ TEM imaging and (B) the corresponding size distribution of the CDs. (C) The UV–vis absorption spectrum, (D) zeta potential, (E) fluorescence spectra at the excitation wavelength 270, 330, and 370 nm, (F) FTIR spectrum, and (G) full XPS spectrum of the CDs. In the XPS spectrum (G), the high-resolution peaks for (H) C1s, (I) O1s, (J) N1s, (K) Na1s, (L) Ca2p, and (M) Cl2p were presented.

(A–C) Fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 490 nm (A), 460 nm (B), and 480 nm (C) with the addition of erythrosine at varied concentrations, with the corresponding linear correlation between the relative fluorescence alteration (F0-F)/F0 and erythrosine level delineated in D, E, and F, respectively. (G and H) Fluorescence intensity of the CDs at the three emission centers with the treatment of different ions, colorants, and amino acids. BG: brilliant green, AB: aniline blue, BPB: bromophenol blue, MG: malachite green, MO: methyl orange, Gly: glycine, Glut: glutamine, Ala: alanine, and Cys: cystine.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs, erythrosine, and the CDs + erythrosine. In addition, the CDs spectrum were physically added with erythrosine spectrum, which was demonstrated as Theoretical. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of erythrosine (Eryt. Abs), and the emission spectra of the CDs at 490, 460, and 480 nm, respectively. (C) The PL decay profiles of CDs in the absence and presence of erythrosine. (D) The relative fluorescence intensity of the CDs treated with erythrosine at various temperatures at 490, 460, and 480 nm, respectively.

Fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 490 nm (A), 460 nm (B), and 480 nm (C) in red wine challenged with erythrosine at diverse concentrations, with the corresponding linear correlation between the relative fluorescence alteration (F0-F)/F0 and erythrosine level described in Figure D, E, and F.

Sensing and imaging of erythrosine in the living cells utilizing the CDs. (A) The confocal images of 4T1 cells, which was first incubated with erythrosine at varying concentrations and then with the CDs. (B) The corresponding fluorescence level of all these cells were determined by flow cytometry. (C) The corresponding statistic analysis of the results in (B).

The effect of (A) temperature, (B) UV–vis irradiation, and (C) NaCl on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 480 nm with the corresponding relative fluorescence histograms presented in Figure D, E and F respectively. The biocompatibility of the CDs was investigated by assessing the viabilities of (G) normal cell L929, and cancer cell 4T1 with the treatment of the CDs at varied concentrations. (I) Hemolysis level of RBCs in the presence of the CDs. RBCs in PBS or Triton X-100 were explored as the negative control or as the positive control.
Triple channel erythrosine detection in red wine, beer, blackcurrant juice, and grapes juice based on the CDs with the emission wavelength of 490 nm, 460 nm, and 480 nm.
The concentrations and the recoveries of erythrosine in all the tested drinks through the standard addition approach using the CDs with the emission wavelength of 490 nm at 270 nm excitation.

head.ce:title: Triple channel fluorescence Na-Ca-Cl-doped carbon dots for erythrosine detection in food samples and living cells

Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Biomaterials and Devices
School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology, Bannu, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Med-X Research Institute, School of Biomedical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200030, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cMed-X Research Institute, School of Biomedical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200030, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is an ongoing and imperative need to monitor food colorants, for unsafe food is a terrifying threat to human health and economy. Here new Na-Ca-Cl-doped carbon dots (CDs) was fabricated by hydrothermal treatment of Miswak and m-phenylenediamine (mPD). The CDs were green-emitting with three distinctive emission centers, displaying a high quantum yield of 47 %. The CDs were able to assess erythrosine in the triple channels based on their fluorescence quenching by erythrosine because of the inner filter effect, displaying high sensitivity and selectivity. Also, the quantitative measurement of erythrosine utilizing the CDs was realized in food samples and in living cells with admirable fluorescence stability and appealing biocompatibility, representing the first report on exploiting CDs for erythrosine sensing in living cells. Collectively, the CDs provide a simple efficient platform for erythrosine sensing in solutions, food samples, and living cells, broadening the implements of CDs as bioprobes for food colorants.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Colorants are broadly leveraged as additives in the industries of food, cosmetics, medicines, and textiles to make products appetizing, attractive, or informative [1]. Erythrosine is a popular synthetic colorant widely existing in food and cosmetics, such as bakery, sweets, custard mix, canned fruits, cherries, and snack foods. Excessive uptake of erythrosine is toxic to human, possibly causing allergies, vomiting, bronchial asthma, renal or thyroid disorder, central nervous system disturbances, and hemolytic disorders [2]. Thus, detection of erythrosine is of great importance for human health. Currently, erythrosine is assessed by spectrophotometry [3], thin layer chromatography [4], high performance liquid chromatography [5], electrospray ionisation–mass spectrometry [6], and electrochemical method [2]. These techniqueuses are usually time-consuming and costly, needing expensive instrument, tedious preconcentration, or highly skilled personnel. Even worse, intracellular detection of erythrosine has not been developed, as far as we know. Therefore, developing simple, inexpensive strategies with high accuracy and selectivity for the determination of erythrosine in food samples and in vivo is highly desired.

Carbon dots (CDs) belong to zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, and possess exceptional properties such as ease of preparation and functionalization, remarkable optical characteristics, superior water solubility, good biocompatibility, surpassing those of organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [7–14]. As a result, CDs have been commonly employed in the fields of bioimaging and bioprobing [15–24]. CDs have been applied in the detection of pesticides [25], food colorants [1,26–28], bisulfite [29], chlorogenic acid [30] in food samples and cosmetics. For example, CDs constructed from eucalyptus twigs were deployed for selective and sensitive assay of brilliant blue [31]. Xu et al. have constructed water-soluble, high-fluorescent CDs from aloe to detect the synthetic food colorant, tartrazine [32]. Ali et al. reported nitrogen-doped CDs (N@C-dots) derived from EDTA sodium salt and glycine for fluorescence measurement of hazardous colorant indigo carmine based on static quenching [33]. Nevertheless, as far as we know, fluorescent CDs for measuring erythrosine has not been reported.

Furthermore, all these CD-based sensors for measuring food colorants possess only single emission center, whose accuracy relies heavily on the intensity change of the single fluorescent center that is inevitably influenced by environmental factors, such as probe concentration and the surrounding detection environment [34]. Sensors with multiple emission centers can resolve these issues and elevate measurement accuracy and robustness [35], attracting increasing attentions. Metal-organic frameworks [36–38], luminescent dyes [39], semiconductor quantum dots [40], and polymer dots [41], have been reported as multi-emission center sensors for ratiometric temperature sensing [36,40,41], fluorescence monitoring of fluoride ion [37], multitarget fluorescence tracing of metal ions, anions, and small molecules [38]. However, these sensors usually require complex synthesis or show high toxicity which has limited their further application [42]. Recently, multi-emission center CDs with simple preparation and good biosafety have raising great interests for bioimaging and biosensing. For instance, Pan et al. constructed CDs from citric acid and formamide through microwave method. Under the excitation at 360, 450, and 540 nm, the CDs displayed three emission peak at 466, 555, and 637 nm respectively, and was employed for cell imaging and as a multidimensional sensing platform to detect various ions including Fe3+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Ce3+, Eu3+, and Cr3+ [43]. In addition, dual-emission CDs have been developed for ratiometric probes for lysine and pH detection [44], cellular pH measurement [45], and peroxynitrite (ONOO−) assay [46]. Nevertheless, the exploration of multi-emission CDs as nanosensors to sensitively and selectively detect food colorants still remains rare.

Herein, CDs were made through the facial and one-step hydrothermal treatment of plant Miswak (Salvadora persica) and m-phenylenediamine (mPD), which were Na-Ca-Cl-doped, and displayed triple emission centres. The as-synthesized CDs were capable of sensitive and selective monitoring of erythrosine, since the triple emission fluorescence of CDs was quenched in the presence of erythrosine because of the inner filter effect. The quantitative assay of erythrosine by the CDs were also achieved in real food samples including red wine, beer, black currant, and grapes juice, and in living cells (Scheme 1), displaying attractive photostability and compelling biocompability. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first-ever study to establish a CD-based bioprobe for triple channel fluorescence detection of food colorant in living cells, offering a simple and economical paradigm for monitoring erythrosine both in vitro and in vivo .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The roots of Miswak and m-phenylenediamine were exploited as the raw materials to synthesize the CDs through a one-pot hydrothermal approach. 100 mg mPD and 1000 mg Miswak powder were added into 30 ml water, which was transferred into a 50 mL stainless-steel Teflon-lined autoclave for being heated for 12 h at 200 °C. After the heating, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and a slightly brownish solution was obtained. The supernatant of the obtained solution was collected by centrifuging for 10 min at 15,000 rpm, which was further filtered against a 0.22 μm filter membrane to get rid of the large particles. Finally, the obtained CD solution was freeze-dried under vacuum overnight and preserved at 4 °C for use in future. The analysis of the CDs was performed regarding its morphology, size, charge, photo properties, components, and fluorescence quantum yield (QY), as reported in our previous study [7]. For more details, please refer to the supporting material.

The CDs was irradiated via UV lamp under the wavelength of 365 nm (20 mW) for varied times, treated with NaCl at varied concentrations (0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 and 2.5 M), or incubated at various temperatures (30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, and 90 °C). Then, the PL intensities of the CDs at 490, 480, and 460 nm at the excitation of 270, 330, and 370 nm respectively were measured via the spectrofluorophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triple channel detection of erythrosine in solutions using the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 30 μL 1 mg/mL CDs were mixed with 2 mM erythrosine of varied volumes, and milliQ water was added to adjust the final volume to be 1 mL, resulting in different final concentrations of erythrosine in each mixture. The fluorescence emission spectra of these mixtures were recorded at 490, 480, and 460 nm under three different excitation wavelengths of 270, 330, and 370 nm. To determine the detection selectivity of the CDs towards erythrosine, the effects of metal ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, and Mg2+), and other colorants (brilliant green, aniline blue, bromophenol blue, methyl green, and methyl orange) on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs were evaluated individually in a similar way to erythrosine. The working curve was mapped via log (F0 -F)/F0 and erythrosine concentration, in which F0 and F were the fluorescence values of the CDs without and with erythrosine respectively.

The detection limit is calculated using the equation LOD=3σ/S, while limits of quantification (LOQ) is determined based on the equation LOQ=10σ/S. σ represents the standard deviation, while S indicates the slope of the linear fit of standard deviation of R2 [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of erythrosine in different drinks
ce:section.ce:para: The application of CDs for erythrosine detection was demonstrated via the standard addition method [7] in drinks including red wine, beer, red grape juice, and black currant juice, which were obtained from the local market in Nanjing, China. Firstly, red wine, beer, red grape juice, and black currant juice were diluted 100 times and filtered through 0.22 μm filters. Then, 2 mM erythrosine stock solution was added in different volumes into the pre-treated drinks to have different final concentrations of erythrosine as indicated in the text, followed by the addition of 30 µL of 1 mg/mL CDs. The final volume of the samples was modulated to 1 mL. Under the excitation of 270 nm, 330 nm and 370 nm, the fluorescence intensities at 490 nm, 460 nm, and 480 nm of these mixtures were taken respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging and sensing of erythrosine in animal cells using the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were grown at 37 °C under 5 % CO2 in 1640 medium with the addition of 10 % fetal bovine serum. A total of 5 × 103 cells per well were transferred into a 96-well plate for overnight incubation and then mixed with 0, 30, 50, 100, and 200 μM erythrosine for 2 h. Then fresh Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (cDMEM) containing 30 μg/mL CDs was used to replace the old culture medium. Following incubation for 30 min, the cell images were taken in the fluorescence emission range of 428–586 nm under the excitation of 405 nm using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica SP8) with a 63 × oil immersion objective. Meanwhile, the flow cytometry was leveraged to measure the fluorescence intensity of the samples in the living cells utilizing the FITC channel.

Fresh mouse blood was collected into an anticoagulation tube to avoid coagulation and then centrifuged for 5 min at 2000 rpm to collect the red blood cells (RBCs) by discarding the serum supernatant. RBCs resuspended in 0.9 % NaCl were mixed with CDs at various final concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, 150, or 200 μg/mL), and then incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. Next, the samples were subjected to centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 5 min in order to obtain the supernatants. The absorbance of these supernatants was recorded at 492 nm on a microplate reader. Positive and negative controls were established using RBCs suspended in 1 % Triton X-100 and PBS, respectively. Below is the formula that was used to calculate the percentage of hemolysis:
H e m o l y s i s % =
(
I
s a m p l e
−
I
n e g a t i v e
)
(
I
p o s i t i v e
c o n t r o l
−
I
n e g a t i v e
c o n t r o l
)
×
100 %

The standard MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) method was deployed to evaluate the in vitro cytotoxicity of the CDs on both normal cells (L929) and cancerous cells (4T1). A total of 5 × 10³ cells per mL were seeded in a 96-well plate and left to adhere overnight. The volume of cells seeded in each well was 100 μL. Following a 24-hour incubation at 37 °C, the culture medium was discarded and the cells were subsequently exposed to Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) for 24 h, and was mixed with varied levels of CDs ranging from 6 to 400 μg/mL. Then, 10 μL 10.0 mg/mL MTT was introduced to each well. Following a 4-hour incubation, the growth medium in each well was replaced with 100 μL DMSO to facilitate the dissolution of MTT. Subsequently, a microplate reader was employed to measure the optical density of the samples at a wavelength of 572 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, optical properties, and chemical composition of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) were made utilizing the hydrothermal treatment of the Miswak roots and m-phenylenediamine (mPD). The TEM analysis of the morphology of the obtained CDs showed that the CDs have good dispersion and irregular shape (Fig. 1A ). The size of the CDs was calculated to be 9.6 ± 0.11 nm by particle size distribution analysis (Nano Measurer 1.2) (Fig. 1B). From the UV–Vis data of the CDs (Fig. 1C), we found a sharp peak at 263 nm that might be described to the π–π* transition of C = C [15], and two small peaks at 330 nm and 542 nm respectively which might be derived from the n− π* transition of C = O and C = N groups [10]. The CDs were green-emitting (Figs. 1E and S1). More intriguingly, they possessed three distinct emission centers at 490, 460 and 480 nm with the excitation at 270, 330, and 370 nm respectively (Fig. 1E). Briefly, the emission intensity of the CDs at 490 nm were the highest when excited at 270 nm (Fig. S1). When excited at 310 nm, the blue shift occurred with the emission peak changing to 460 nm. The fluorescence intensity reached the maximal value with the excitation at 330 nm and then reduced under the excitation at 350 nm. Next, when the excitation wavelength was 390 nm, the red shifting happened with the emission peak switching to 480 nm, showing the highest intensity, which then fell gradually as the excitation wavelength was altered from 390 nm to 490 nm. The existence of multiple emission peaks of the CDs were believed to be derived from varied functionalities on the CDs surface and the size effect [47]. The CDs possessed a charge of +5 mV (Fig. 1D). In addition, the quantum yield (QY) of the CDs in water was recorded to be 47 %, which far surpassed that of the reported plant-based CDs [48,49]. In summary, green-emitting positively-charged CDs with irregular shape were successfully prepared with the high quantum yield of 47 %, showing three distinctive emission centers at 490, 460 and 480 nm with the excitation at 270, 330, and 370 nm respectively.

To determine their composition, the synthesized CDs were analyzed through fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS). The two broad absorption bands at 3412 cm-1 and 3550 cm-1 in the FTIR spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 1F) can be attributed to the bending vibration of N–H and O–H individually. The peak at 2941 cm-1 can be ascribed to the C–H bending vibration. The peak at 1718 cm-1 can be due to the stretching vibration of C = O that is likely in the groups of –COOH or –CONH–. The peak at 1622 cm-1 can be owing to the C = C stretching vibration. The peaks at 1439 cm−1, 1119 cm−1, 873 cm-1, and 678 cm-1 may respectively stem from the stretching vibration of CN, C–O–C, Ca-O, and CCl.

In addition to FTIR, XPS analysis of the elemental components and surface groups of the CDs were performed (Fig. 1G). The CDs were composed of 74 % carbon, 20.7 % oxygen, 3.4 % nitrogen, 0.66 % sodium, 0.54 % calcium, and 0.47 % chlorine, indicating that the CDs was doped with elements Na, Ca, and Cl. Six peaks were observed in the full XPS spectrum of the CDs at 197.3 eV, 284.5 eV, 347.0 eV, 399.2 eV, 531.7 eV, and 1070.6 eV for Cl2p, C1s, Ca2p, N1s, O1s, and Na1s, respectively (Fig. 1G). Three distinctive peaks were found in the C1s band, including 284.4 eV, 285.3 eV, and 288.4 eV that were related to carbon sp2 (C = C), carbon sp3 (C–N), and carbon sp3 (CO) (Fig. 1H) individually. In the O1s spectrum, the two fitted peaks at 531.4 and 532.7 eV can be assigned to the oxygen element in the forms of CO and C = O, respectively (Fig. 1I). The N1s band contained three distinct peaks that were related to the pyridine N (CN-C, 399.0 eV), the amino N (NC, 399.9 eV), and the pyrrole N (CNH2, 401.1 eV) (Fig. 1J). The peak at 1070.6 eV in the Na1s spectrum can be owing to Na-O (Fig. 1K). The Ca2p band had two Gaussian peaks at 346.8 eV and 350.2 eV that were ascribed to Ca 2p1/2 and Ca 2p3/2 bands, respectively (Fig. 1L), while the Cl2p band were differentiated into two peaks at 197.0 and 198.6 eV attributed to the binding energies of Cl2p1/2 and Cl2p3/2 respectively owing to the spin–orbit splitting of the Cl2p core level (1.6 eV) (Fig. 1M) [50]. According to the FTIR and XPS data, we know that the CDs contained elements Na, Ca, and Cl, and was functionalized with the carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triple-channel fluorescence sensing of erythrosine utilizing the CDs in various solutions with high selectivity
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, we found that the fluorescence intensity at all the three fluorescence emission centers of the CDs was quenched by erythrosine, a common food colorant (Fig. 2 ), indicating that the CDs can be harnessed as a triple channel probe to measure erythrosine. The fluorescent intensity of the CDs at 490 nm, 460 nm, and 480 nm were gradually reduced when the erythrosine concentrations rose from 10 µM to 60 µM (Fig. 2A), 80 µM to 250 µM (Fig. 2B), and 80 µM to 250 µM (Fig. 2C), showing a linear range of 10–60 µM (Fig. 2D), 80–250 µM (Fig. 2E), and 80–200 µM (Fig. 2F) with the detection limit of 6.15 nM, 25.0 nM, and 29.9 nM, respectively. The performance of the CDs for erythrosine assay was comparable with that of the currently available erythrosine detection methods based on capillary electrophoresis with laser induced fluorescence [51], differential pulse voltammetry [2], resonance rayleigh scattering [52], glassy carbon electrode [53], and square wave voltammetry [54] in terms of the detection limit (Table S1). However, it is worth noting that none of these reported methods were capable of detecting erythrosine intracellularly. Furthermore, these existing erythrosine detection approaches require expensive instruments or complicated assay procedures [2,51–54], while the fluorescence detection of erythrosine on the basis of CDs might offer simpler and more cost-effective alternative.

To study the selectivity of the triple-channel CD-derived fluorescence method, the fluorescence intensity of CDs with the addition of the possible interfering substances including various kinds of ions (Fe3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, CH3COO-, Cl-, CO3 2-, HPO4 2-, SO4 2-, HCO3 -), colorants (brilliant green, aniline blue, bromophenol blue, malachite green, and methyl orange), and different amino acids (glycine, glutamine, alanine, and cystine), which might be found in food samples and living cells, were investigated (Fig. 2G and 2H). It can be seen that only erythrosine can markedly quench the fluorescence of the CDs, while the quenching effects of the other possible interferences were almost negligible or not that significant (Fig. 2G and 2H), indicating that the CDs have high selectivity towards erythrosine over the possible interfering substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible detection mechanism for erythrosine using the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We explored the possible mechanism of the CDs for sensing erythrosine. The theoretical UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs/erythrosine mixture, which was obtained by physically summing up the spectra of the CDs and erythrosine, marched well with the experimental one (Fig. 3A ), dissecting that the interaction between erythrosine and the CDs is too weak to generate complexes, making the electron transfer between erythrosine and the CDs impossible. As a result, the fluorescence quenching of the CDs by erythrosine was not because of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [55]. Meanwhile, the absorption peak of erythrosine at 400–600 nm overlapped with the three emission centers of the CDs at 490, 460, and 480 nm (Fig. 3B), implying that the observed fluorescence quenching of the CDs by erythrosine may be attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or the inner filter effect (IFE) [56–58]. Fluorescence lifetime measurements provide useful insights into the quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence decay curves of the CDs in the absence and presence of erythrosine are almost identical, with lifetimes of 6.62 ns and 6.59 ns, respectively. This observation rules out FRET as the underlying quenching mechanism. If FRET was occurring, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs would decrease noticeably upon the addition of erythrosine. The negligible change in lifetime evidenced that IFE is likely responsible for the fluorescence quenching of the CDs [59,60]. Meanwhile, temperature did not affect the fluorescence of the CDs at all three-emission wavelengths of 490, 460, and 480 nm (Fig. 3D), further supporting IFE. All together, IFE is probably the quenching mechanism for the CDs at all three emission centers in the presence of erythrosine.

Specifically, the fluorescence quenching through IFE occurs when a substance absorbs light at wavelengths that overlap with the excitation or emission wavelengths of a fluorophore [61,62]. Erythrosine absorbs light in the visible region, specifically around 525 nm. This absorption spectrum overlaps with the emission wavelengths of the CDs, which are centered at 490 nm, 460 nm, and 480 nm. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at these emission centers was decreased in the presence of erythrosine, leading to the fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample detection of erythrosine in red wine, grapes juice, beer, and blackcurrant juice
ce:section.ce:para: Additionally, we determined the concentrations and the recoveries of erythrosine in all the tested drinks by the standard addition method (Tables 2 , S2, and S3). The triple-channel erythrosine detection method on the basis of the CDs enjoys good recoveries ranging from 94.0 % to 107.0 %, with the relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) varying from 0.2 % to 5.0 % in all the studied drinks, manifesting the high analytical precision of our method that is adequate for quantitative measurement in real samples. Therefore, this method can accurately determine erythrosine in food samples and drinks, which will provide strong support for food safety monitoring with a high degree of simplicity and accuracy. In addition, integration of the CDs with paper strips to develop test kits is worthy exploring in future study, which will make the erythrosine assay convenient and user-friendly, presenting a forward step toward the commercialization of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the CDs for intracellular erythrosine detection
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we explored whether the CDs can be harnessed to monitor erythrosine in living cells, which is necessary and important to monitor erythrosine in human when consuming erythrosine-containing food. As shown by CLSM (Fig. 5 ), the CDs successfully entered 4T1 cells, making the CDs possible to trace erythrosine in the living cells. The fluorescence of the CDs in 4T1 cells was decreased noticeably, when erythrosine concentration was elevated from 0 to 200 μM (Fig. 5A). Besides CLSM, the CDs can quantitatively monitor cellular erythrosine through flow cytometry (Fig. 5B and 5C). As the erythrosine level in 4T1 cancer cells was boosted, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in these cells determined by flow cytometry, was decreased steadily, which was in line with the CLSM results (Fig. 5A). These data show that the CDs can be applied for the tracking of erythrosine not only in solutions and drinks, but also in living cells.

The photostability of the CDs crucial for practical implements were determined under varied experimental conditions such as temperature, UV irradiation, and ions at all three emission channels (Fig. 6A –6C). When the temperature was elevated from 28 °C to 95 °C, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 490 nm (Fig. 6A and 6D), 460 nm (Fig. S7A and S7D), and 480 nm (Fig. S8A and S8D) stayed constant. When irradiated by the UV of 365 nm for time periods in the range of 10–360 min, no change was found in the PL of the CDs at all the three emission centers (Figs. 6B, 6E, S7B, S7E, S8B, and S8E). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at all the three emission channels remained unchanged even when NaCl concentration reached 5.0 M (Figs. 6C, 6F, S7C, S7F, S8C, and S8F). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs stayed constant during pH 3–9, and was decreased under basic (pH 10–12) conditions (Fig. S7). All these results suggest that the CDs display high PL stability at all the three channels, which is good for their practical utilization.

Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of the CDs was investigated through MTT approach and hemolysis. MTT data showed that the relative viabilities of normal cells (L929) were more than 93 % when treated with 400 μg/mL CDs, while more than 82 % cancer cells (4T1) cell were alive in the presence of the CDs with concentrations from 0 to 40 μg/mL (Fig. 6G and 6H), implicating that the CDs are nontoxic at the concentrations (no more than 30 μg/mL) used for erythrosine tracing. Moreover, even when the concentration of the CDs was up to 150 μg/mL, the hemolytic effect of the CDs on RBCs was still insignificant (Fig. 6I), indicating the CDs possess excellent hemocompatibility. These observations demonstrate that the CDs have appealing photostability and satisfactory biocompatibility which is good for their application in erythrosine monitoring both in vitro and in vivo.

Miswak is abundant in nature, which is cost-effective and environmentally- friendly. It has been traditionally utilized to make toothpaste and should be biocompatible. It contains cellulose and lignin, contributing to the formation of carbon dots. m-Phenylenediamine (mPD) is a well-known nitrogen source that enhances the optical properties of carbon dots through nitrogen doping, such as high quantum yields and good photo stability [63,64]. The use of Miswak and mPD as the raw materials resulted in the CDs with high quantum yield (47 %), excellent photo stability (Fig. 6D–6F), and great biocompatibility (Fig. 6G–6I)), benefiting its implement as the effective fluorescence sensor for erythrosine detection both in vitro and in vivo.

Developing robust large-scale synthetic procedure for the production of the CD is crucial for their applications. Although studies on the synthesis of kilogram-scale CDs has been reported [65–70]. Nevertheless, some challenges might arise in scaling up the production of the CDs, including quality consistency, efficient purification approaches, and cost-effective synthesis processes. First, attention should be paid to effects of scaling-up and washing process on the properties of the CDs to achieve good quality control of the CDs with high reproducibility. Second, it is necessary to develop adequate purification techniques to obtain relatively pure CDs in large scale to exclude the impact of impurities, and to remove the hazards from unreacted precursors and solvents. Furthermore, the washing process for large scale production might consume a large amount of solvent that might count a lot in doing a techno-economic analysis [3]. Third, the cost of the process for large-scale production of the CDs should be evaluated. Besides the raw materials themselves, cost of labor, energy consumption, solvent recycle, waste management, and environment influence should be taken into consideration.

The development of simple, inexpensive assays with great accuracy and high selectivity for the determination of food colorants is highly desired. Here, in this study we have successfully developed novel carbon dots (CDs) through a one-step hydrothermal treatment of plant Miswak and mPD. These green-emitting CDs possess three distinctive emission centers with a high quantum yield of 47 % and were determined to be doped with Na, Ca, and Cl elements. In addition, the CDs were able to rapidly monitor erythrosine in triple channels with good linear ranges and low detection limits, while also exhibiting great sensitivity and selectivity. These CDs were also successfully used for erythrosine detection in various drinks and living cells, demonstrating their potential for sensitive detection in different applications. This study is the first to report the use of CDs for fluorescence probing of erythrosine in live cells, filling a gap in the field of in-situ intracellular erythrosine monitoring. Overall, the developed CDs present a promising approach for sensitive and selective detection of erythrosine in solutions, food, and living cells. Moreover, the good biocompatibility and cellular uptake of these CDs suggest they could be explored as carriers for drug delivery, or as fluorescence sensors for cell imaging, in addition to the potential application in anti-counterfeiting technologies for product authentication.

Samran Durrani: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jie Zhang: Validation, Methodology. Faisal Durrani: Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology. Zihao Wang: Validation. Mukramin: Validation. Ke-Fei Xu: Validation. Haiyan Wang: Validation. Haroon Khan: Validation. Fu-Gen Wu: Validation, Resources. Fengming Lin: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32170072), Jiangsu Province Excellent Post-Doctoral Program, and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.139934.
